title: Cum au trecut românii testul Războiului din Ucraina
summary: The article examines how Romanians and their political leaders have responded to the Ukraine war as it enters its fifth year, noting declining public support for Ukraine and growing moral relativism among politicians. It criticizes President Nicușor Dan and his advisor Marius Lazurca for ambiguous positions toward Russia, and highlights the spread of Russian propaganda through academic institutions and political parties like AUR.
talking_points: Romanian public support for Ukraine has significantly declined — those believing Russia should return occupied territories dropped from 64.7% to 53.3%, and 42.6% oppose any aid to Ukraine

President Nicușor Dan's advisor Marius Lazurca promotes moral relativism toward Russia, arguing against framing the war in moral terms and advocating that Europe leave Ukraine negotiations to the US and Russia
Dan's participation in Trump's Board of Peace inauguration in Washington, where he held a MAGA hat, symbolizes Romania's drift toward Trumpism and away from European strategic coordination
Russian propaganda penetrates Romanian academic institutions, including the University of Bucharest and the Romanian Academy, where pro-Russian figures operate state-funded centers
The political class, especially PSD, has capitulated to anti-Ukrainian propaganda from AUR and SOS, including blocking Zelensky from addressing Romania's Parliament
related_concepts: Russian propaganda and influence in EU member states
author: Rodica Culcer
